name: 2° AV Block Mobitz I (Wenckebach)
aliases: Second Degree AV Block Type I
identifyBy: Progressive PR interval prolongation with each beat
Followed by a dropped QRS (non-conducted P wave)
Shortest PR after the dropped beat, then progressively lengthens
Regular PP interval but irregular RR (due to dropped beat)
Group beating pattern: '3 P waves with 2 QRS complexes' etc.

measurements.pWaves: More P waves than QRS complexes

management: Usually benign, often no treatment required if asymptomatic
Symptomatic: Atropine 0.6mg IV (responds well - nodal block)
Identify and treat cause: inferior MI, drug toxicity, vagal excess, myocarditis
Monitor for progression to complete heart block (rare with Mobitz I)
pearls: Wenckebach is an AV node (nodal) block and responds to Atropine (unlike Mobitz II)
Inferior MI + Wenckebach: common, usually transient, good prognosis
Shorter cycles: the increment of PR prolongation gets smaller each beat (though PR keeps getting longer)
The most common and benign form of second-degree AV block
Distinguish from Mobitz II: Mobitz I has narrow QRS and gradual PR prolongation; Mobitz II has wide QRS and sudden drop
differentials: Mobitz II (sudden dropped QRS, constant PR, wide QRS)
Complete heart block (no P waves conduct)
Blocked PAC (premature P wave not conducted)
